data.name: Mathew Ryan
data.description: You could say the Socceroos captain has been around the block. The 33-year-old has played for 12 clubs across eight countries – including Club Brugge, Brighton and Alkmaar, and even brief spells at Arsenal and Roma – in a career spanning more than 15 years. Now appearing at his fourth World Cup, the boy from Plumpton in Sydney’s west is the latest in a long line of quality Australian keepers, including Mark Bosnich and Zeljko Kalac. Another one of his predecessors, Mark Schwarzer, was his childhood hero and a teenage Ryan was at Sydney’s Olympic Stadium in 2005 to witness Schwarzer and co famously beat Uruguay on penalties to qualify for Australia’s first World Cup in 32 years. Ryan now leads Australia to their sixth straight World Cup but in an alternate universe, he could be wearing a Three Lions shirt in North America – he also holds a British passport.

data.name: Miloš Degenek
data.description: A cross-border transfer in the year of a World Cup will always make fans nervous, but if there is a player who can pull it off then it surely must be Degenek. His move from Serbia to Cyprus with Apoel means he has now played in seven different countries, including the US. The muscular and composed central defender has appeared for the Socceroos more than 50 times, although he has never been a first XI lock. A serious heel injury in 2023 threatened to end his high-level career, but he returned to the Socceroos setup last year. In camp in November, he told the ABC winning a World Cup wasn’t hard to get your head around. "You get on the plane, you fly back to your base, and you go again and again,” he said. “And then in July, we all fly back to Australia and celebrate the World Cup”.

data.name: Alessandro Circati
data.description: In the dressing room at Parma several years ago, Circati walked up to the goalkeeper Gianluigi Buffon – who helped ruin the Socceroos’ World Cup dreams in 2006 – seeking advice. The teenager had to choose between Italy, his country of birth, and Australia, where he grew up, over who he would represent at international level. “What do you feel inside? Forget about everything else,” Buffon said, according to a Circati interview with ESPN. The young defender chose the Socceroos and, despite losing most of a year to an anterior cruciate ligament injury in 2024, has already captained his national side by the age of 22. As a composed and skilful centre-back already proven in Serie A, Circati comes into the 2026 World Cup as the Socceroos’ most polished defender. There may be no one in the Australia squad more important than him to the coach, Tony Popovic.

data.name: Jacob Italiano
data.description: After making his Socceroos debut against Canada in 2025, Italiano said it had been “a long journey” to get his first cap. But really, he was only 24 and the wait only felt so long because he packed his bags to join the youth pathway a whole decade earlier. Known for his pace and skill moving the ball up the wing, Italiano is also a versatile player, as comfortable in an attacking midfield position as he is at left-back or right-back. Despite calling Borussia Mönchengladbach home for five years, Italiano never got his chance to play in the German Bundesliga, instead racking up more than 100 appearances for the reserves. But things are looking up after a move across the border – Italiano is now a regular starter for Graz in the Austrian Bundesliga. With Lewis Miller suffering an achilles injury, Popovic may look to Italiano to step up.

data.name: Jordan Bos
data.description: The attacking left-back has continued his steady rise from Melbourne City to Feyenoord while gradually becoming a key creative outlet for the national side. The Eredivisie giants have a long history of providing a home for Australia internationals and Bos has used the added advantage of Dutch heritage to not only settle quickly but make a stunning start to life in the Netherlands. Bos lifts the intensity of those around him with his pace and tireless running, and loves getting high up the pitch, overlapping relentlessly and delivering early crosses into the box. Despite his light frame the 23-year-old is prepared to get stuck into a tackle, though his lack of defensive awareness can cost him at times. But with his high energy and enthusiasm just what Popovic expects from his players, Bos could spend time on a wing where his better traits are more effective.

data.name: Jason Geria
data.description: A favourite of Ange Postecoglu, who oversaw his development at Brisbane Roar, Melbourne Victory and the national setup, Geria started his career as a member of the Australian Institute of Sport football programme in Canberra before joining Brisbane’s youth team in 2011. A year later Postecoglou signed Geria at Victory, where he became a regular name on the team sheet. Geria played for Australia multiple times at youth level before Postecoglou (again) called him into the Socceroos squad bidding to reach the 2018 World Cup. He made his debut in a friendly against Greece in 2016 and eight years later, after stints in Japan and with multiple A-League clubs, Tony Popovic recalled him to the Socceroos squad for 2026 World Cup qualification.

data.name: Mathew Leckie
data.description: Leckie made the 23-man squad for the Russia World Cup in 2018, but it was at the tournament in Qatar four years later when he cemented himself as a Socceroos cult hero. The versatile winger provided one of Australian football’s all-time moments when he jinked inside the Denmark defender Joakim Mæhle, then back outside, before firing low past the despairing dive of Kasper Schmeichel to score the only goal of the game and send the Socceroos into the last 16 – and fans assembled in the early hours back at home into raptures. Now 35, this will be Leckie’s fourth World Cup. At his first in 2014 his explosive acceleration and direct running made him one of Australia’s standout players and attracted interest from scouts from several big clubs, although he has always maintained he had no regrets over signing for the German second division club Ingolstadt a month earlier.

data.name: Connor Metcalfe
data.description: One of Australia’s quiet achievers, the Melbourne City academy product initially burst on to the scene and captained the club when aged only 22 before making the move abroad. Metcalfe joined Jackson Irvine, the national-team leader, at St Pauli in 2022 and was key to the club’s promotion in his second season in Germany. A technical and tenacious midfielder, Metcalfe presses aggressively, is deceptively strong in a tackle and increasingly confident in dictating the tempo with the ball. But he is more likely to let his football do the talking, while letting teammates such as Irvine instead fit in with their club’s social conscience. “It's pretty cool, all punk rock and that,” Metcalfe has said about playing for St Pauli. “I'm not punk rock one bit. I’m basic. Basic as. Short back and sides, that’s what we can call it.”

data.name: Mohamed Touré
data.description: “If he carries on at this rate, he’ll be the greatest striker of all time.” So said one online comment after Australia’s latest next big thing announced himself to the Championship with a hat-trick on his full debut and a total of five goals in his first four appearances for Norwich. Touré was born in a Guinean refugee camp before moving to South Australia at a young age, and he counts two brothers – Al-Hasan and Musa – as fellow professional players, while his dad, uncle and sister have also played at a high level. After making his Socceroos debut at Wembley in October 2023 he said: “You have to thank your parents, the struggles they go through when you’re younger to get us to playing. Sometimes we forget where it all started and who actually put in the work from the start.” The family lived opposite a football pitch and that’s where the Touré boys spent hours honing their ball skills together. A debut for Adelaide United followed at the age of 15 before he headed to Europe, where his continued development first attracted the interest of Tony Popovic.

data.name: Ajdin Hrustic
data.description: The versatile midfielder continues to tease as the playmaker that Australia is too often left crying out for as he battles the highs and lows of an injury-plagued career. Hrustic can make the game look easy with his rhythm on the ball and dazzling feet, defence-splitting passes and a powerful left boot when in sight of goal. His set pieces remain a threat for club and country, most memorably shown when he unleashed a thunderbolt against Japan during qualification for the last World Cup. Born in Melbourne to a Romanian mother and a Bosnian father, Hrustic is fluent in multiple languages, which helped him step off the Australian player pathways at the age of 14 and try his luck in Europe. He has since played for clubs in England, Austria, Germany, the Netherlands and Italy, and is now bringing his creative spark to the Dutch top tier.

data.name: Awer Mabil
data.description: It’s not often a footballer’s off-field feats match those on it, but Mabil is a rare case. Born to South Sudanese parents in the Kakuma refugee camp in Kenya, Mabil started playing football at the age of five, without footwear and using a rolled-up sock as a ball. His experiences before leaving the camp for Adelaide at the age of 10 shaped the person he is today and his humanitarian work as an adult earned him the young Australian of the year award in 2023. His foundation, Barefoot to Boots, is a not-for-profit organisation which aims for better health, education, policies and gender equality for refugees. On the pitch, Mabil made his debut for the Socceroos in 2018 and has gone on to make 38 appearances, scoring 10 goals, at the time of writing.

data.name: Paul Izzo
data.description: A few years ago, Izzo leaned on his car’s horn and sang the name of Bruno Fornaroli out the window as his then Melbourne Victory teammate received a surprise call-up to the Asian Cup. Now, he is off to the World Cup himself. Becoming a regular part of squads after Tony Popovic’s ascension to the Socceroos coaching role – having previously played for him at Xanthi and Victory – Izzo made his full international debut against New Zealand post-qualification and hasn’t looked back since, making a record eight saves in a man-of-the-match performance against Canada the following month. Ryan’s form in La Liga means he is probably the first-choice, but it is testament to Izzo’s rise that he has made that a question at all during the buildup to the tournament.

data.name: Aiden O'Neill
data.description: The combative midfielder brings balance and bite to his national and club teams as he plays with an edge first and elegance second. Born and raised in Brisbane, O’Neill found his feet as a teenager in the lower leagues in England before returning to Australia on loan with his hometown A-League club. His potential truly started to shine through after a move to Melbourne City where he became the captain and a midfield enforcer. O’Neill points, barks and organises the team from the centre of the pitch and took on another leadership role with Belgium club Standard Liège before moving to New York City last year. O’Neill is now setting standards in the Big Apple while occasionally allowing his mind to wander to home comforts. “I own a farm as well, that's something I'm pretty passionate about,” he said. “We run cattle there, so that's a big part of my life off the field.”

data.name: Cammy Devlin
data.description: At the time of writing Devlin is yet to see the field for the Socceroos during the Tony Popovic era, but his irrepressible tenacity in midfield as Hearts challenged the Glasgow duopoly on Scottish football has proven impossible for the coach to ignore for World Cup selection. After bouncing around both his hometown clubs in Western Sydney and Sydney FC, Devlin experienced his A-League Men breakout with Wellington in 2019. However, it wasn't the Phoenix who netted a fee for his move to Scotland; that would be the Newcastle Jets, who signed him as a free agent in late June 2021, only for him to then move to Edinburgh two months later.

data.name: Kai Trewin
data.description: The ball-playing defender has played with a maturity well beyond his years since a debut for Brisbane as an 18-year-old. He soon cemented his place with the Roar as a composed centre-back who is strong in one-on-one contests and comfortable stepping into a defensive midfield role. A move to Melbourne City has proved to be a stepping stone to joining their sister club in the US as he builds valuable experience that should hold him in good stead with the national team. He made surprise headlines in the buildup to this summer after he became the latest player to be caught up in an Instagram trend that aimed to make relatively low-profile World Cup squad members famous before the tournament began. Trewin had 3,000 followers before being singled out; he now has nearly 130,000.

data.name: Aziz Behich
data.description: Amid a surfeit of loping defenders and towering backline hybrids available to Popovic, Behich zipping down the left is a comforting reminder of the diminutive full-backs of yore. He appears to have put a hamstring injury last year behind him, and his zest for the game and zeal for the contest means he will compete with Jordy Bos to start for the Socceroos in North America. There may be some Euro-centric snobbery among Socceroos fans, but it is a testament to the quality of Behich that he has remained an enduring member of the national team despite choosing to return home to play in the A-League Men in 2023. No player took part in more of the Socceroos’ World Cup qualifiers than Behich, who was also recognised by his peers by being named Professional Footballers Australia's men's footballer of the year for 2025.

data.name: Nestory Irankunda
data.description: Irankunda “couldn’t stop smiling” when he scored his first Socceroos goal in 2024. His young shoulders bear a weight no other Socceroos player can know – unhesitatingly hefted upon him by the Australian media the moment he started finding the back of the net for Adelaide United as a 15-year-old in 2022. The former national coach Graham Arnold sought to shield him from the expectations associated with the label of “most exciting young Australian footballer ever”, but the winger’s confidence and skill with the ball at his feet and his eye for a spectacular goal have made it impossible to dampen the excitement. After a record-breaking move from the A-League to Bayern Munich in late 2024, Irankunda then signed with Watford in July 2025, scoring his first goal a month later from a free-kick outside the area in the very style that earned him the weighty tag to begin with. Tony Popovic has a star in his hands – and his challenge is to help it shine at a World Cup.

data.name: Patrick Beach
data.description: A former softball player, the 22-year-old Sydneysider was a defender until he reached the under-14 level, when he asked to make the switch to between the posts. Starting in the New South Wales development programme at Mount Druitt Town Rangers, Beach headed overseas to trial at teams in Northern Ireland and Ireland before a final trial at Premier League-bound Ipswich Town. Eventually signing with the Central Coast Mariners in 2023, it wasn't until he was picked up by Melbourne City in 2024 that he made his A-League debut. Within 12 months he was an A-League champion. In March 2025, he earned a first call-up to a Socceroos camp before later that year making his debut against Venezuela – earning man-of-the-match honours at the first time of asking.

data.name: Harry Souttar
data.description: “You’ve got to love Souttar. You’ve got to absolutely love him.” So said Pat Nevin after a particularly impressive outing from the towering centre-back in a hard-fought 1-0 win over Denmark at the 2022 World Cup. Many agree with the Scottish pundit and it’s easy to understand why Souttar became an instant fan favourite after making his debut for Australia in 2019. Two goals in that game against Nepal were followed by another two in his second full international v Chinese Taipei (remarkably he has a total of 11 in 36 games at the time of writing). But it is not just his goal threat that has observers purring – a no-nonsense, body-on-the-line playing style made him a regular starter under the previous coach, Graham Arnold, before an achilles injury disrupted his preparations for North America. Born in Aberdeen, he qualifies for the Socceroos through his Western Australia-born mother and, at two meters, he has the distinction of being the tallest outfield player to have played for Australia. Only the goalkeeper Zeljco Kalac has been taller.

data.name: Cristian Volpato
data.description: The prodigious talent ended years of speculation over whether he would commit to Australia – the country of his birth – or Italy with accepting a bombshell call-up to the Socceroos squad on the eve of this World Cup. The previous head coach, Graham Arnold, said Volpato had turned down the chance to play at the 2022 tournament in Qatar, which came a few months after the player appeared to mock the Socceroos on Snapchat following a 2-0 defeat to Japan in Asian Cup qualifying. The 22-year-old can play as an attacking midfielder or winger with his versatility helping to establish him as a regular at Sassuolo over the past three seasons – and earn him selection with Italy’s youth teams in recent years. “When you have a talented player, I'm not sure what the risk is,” the Socceroos coach, Tony Popovic, said of the selection curveball. Now it’s over to the Sydney-born and raised Volpato to live up to the hype.

data.name: Cameron Burgess
data.description: Late last year, a defender’s worst nightmare befell the 30-year-old. Playing against his former club Ipswich, a luckless Burgess found the back of his own net, not once, but twice. It prompted him to issue a statement to Swansea fans. “As a player, I hear your criticism and you won’t hear any excuses from me,” he wrote. “I am my own worst critic, believe me, and it’s my sole purpose to put things right on the pitch.” To be fair, both own goals could be considered unlucky, but his reaction spoke to the man Burgess has become, having started his professional career at Fulham before making several moves around the lower reaches of English football and landing at Portman Road, where he helped Ipswich to successive promotions from League One to the Premier League. Another of the Australian contingent to have been born in Scotland – he made the move down under at age 11 – Burgess knows his place in the starting XI in North America is not guaranteed. “Every time you get the chance to play, it’s a real honour, and something none of us take for granted,” he said.

data.name: Jackson Irvine
data.description: The Australia midfield leader plays with the calm authority and tactical nous of someone who has seen the grind of the lower leagues and now relishes playing on the grandest stages for club and country. Born and raised in Melbourne, battle-hardened in Scotland and England, Irvine is thriving in Germany. Since arriving at St Pauli in 2021, he has become the heartbeat of their midfield, the club captain, and a key player in their return to the German top tier. The promotion celebrations were so wild that Irvine forgot to take his passport to his own wedding two days later. The otherwise thoughtful and articulate Socceroo is not afraid to express his political and social views and, in April, hit out at Fifa for awarding a peace prize to Donald Trump. “As an organisation, you would have to say decisions like the one that we saw awarding this peace prize makes a mockery of what they’re trying to do with the human rights charter,” he said. Yet a lighter side hides in plain sight, with Irvine’s many tattoos including one on his right leg of Simpsons character Moe Szyslak.

data.name: Nishan Velupillay
data.description: Having not played a part in the past four international windows, Velupillay is something of a bolter in Tony Popovic’s squad, and admitted recently he has missed the Socceroos environment. “I feel like you always get an extra boost of confidence when you come in here,” he said. The one-club man burst on to the scene for Melbourne Victory in 2021, becoming a regular first-team starter and winning the club’s young player of the year award. Velupillay worked his way into the Australian Under-23s setup before being handed a chance to impress in a Socceroos World Cup qualifying squad; he went on to score in back-to-back games against China and Indonesia last year.

data.name: Paul Okon-Engstler
data.description: Son of the former Socceroos player and now assistant Paul Okon, Okon-Engstler is eager to create his own legacy while remaining mindful of the influence his father has had on his career. "He's always been such a big inspiration for me and for my brothers ... anything to do with football, he's honest,” Okon-Engstler says. “He gives his honest opinion and what he thinks we need to improve on.” The 21-year-old played youth football at Club Brugge in Belgium before he made the move to the Portuguese giants Benfica, making eight appearances for their second team before securing a move to Sydney FC in 2025. In the US and Canada, he will try to put the disappointment of the Sky Blues’ recent A-League grand final defeat by Auckland behind him.

data.name: Lucas Herrington
data.description: The youngest player selected in Australia’s World Cup squad at 18, Herrington has already tested himself against several of the game’s greats during his fledgling career – since signing for Colorado in MLS, he has counted Lionel Messi, Rodrigo De Paul, Thomas Müller and Son Heung-min among his opponents. Herrington started his career at Brisbane Roar, where he was crowned their young player of the year after an impressive debut season. He became the youngest player since Nestroy Irankunda to debut for Australia, with appearances in friendlies against Cameroon and Curaçao this year, describing the experience of representing his country as “the best feeling in the world". The young Queenslander shapes as one of Socceroos brightest prospects and one to keep an eye on.

data.name: Tete Yengi
data.description: There is a fair amount of excitement about Yengi, despite his having largely flown under the radar before being called in at the last minute to the Socceroos pre-World Cup training camp in Sarasota. For many years the 1.98m (6ft 6in) striker might not even have been the best attacker in his family, with his brother Kusini taking that mantle, but this year he has leapfrogged his older brother and staked his claim for national-team selection. On loan to the Japanese first division team Machida Zelvia from Livingston, Yengi helped his side reach the Asian Champions League Elite final, where he enjoyed a moment of internet fame after being headbutted by the much shorter Al Ahli defender Zakaria al-Hawsawi.
